idea.hook: How big can *nothing* get in space?
facts: The Milky Way galaxy is ~100,000 light-years across.
Our Local Group of galaxies (including Milky Way and Andromeda) spans ~10 million light-years.
The Boötes Void, a region of extreme emptiness, is an astonishing 330 million light-years in diameter.
idea.payoff: This single void is large enough to contain THOUSANDS of Milky Way-sized galaxies, yet holds only about 60!
idea.title: The Universe's Biggest Void! 🤯🌌

scenes.text: How big can NOTHING get in space?
scenes.visual: Vast, dark cosmic expanse with faint, distant galaxies twinkling. Subtle cosmic dust motes.
Vibrant, detailed image of the Milky Way galaxy, slowly rotating. A small scale bar appears briefly.
An illustration showing the Milky Way, Andromeda, and other smaller galaxies in proximity, depicting the 'Local Group'. Zooming out slightly to reveal more empty space around them.
An overwhelming expanse of almost complete darkness. A faint, large circular outline briefly appears, highlighting the immense size of the void against tiny, sparse galaxy dots.

scenes.text: Void big enough for THOUSANDS of galaxies... yet holds ~60!
scenes.visual: The dark void dominates the screen. Small dots representing the ~60 galaxies are faintly visible, emphasizing their extreme isolation within the vast emptiness. A counter showing '60' briefly appears and fades.
